Just now

--

For most of human history, the barrier to building a business wasn’t the idea. It was everything else.
Before you could test whether the market wanted what you had, you needed a lawyer to form the company, a designer to make it look legitimate, a marketer who understood the channels, a developer if any of it lived online. You needed capital upfront — not to produce the thing, but just to assemble the team capable of producing the thing. Most people with genuinely good ideas never got past that list. The idea died quietly, and the people who controlled access to those services collected their fees and moved on.
That structure is collapsing.
I know this not as theory but as recent personal experience. I’ve been building a business from a small town in north-central Washington, the kind of place where you don’t have a professional network to call on, where the nearest relevant expert might be hours away. A few years ago, what I’m doing now would have required capital I didn’t have, and connections I’d never built. Instead, I’ve staffed the operation largely with AI agents — tools that helped me form the company, develop the products, think through positioning, draft the copy. The things people used to pay thousands of dollars for, and often had to wait…
